text: Siakam at the elbow and more touches in space. Haliburton as a screener and off ball more so he can catch the ball in space and mess with OKC's switching. Staggered screens into PNR with the weakest guard. Haliburton rejecting screens more, more aggressive earlier and just taking advantage of how aggressively OKC help. Doubling SGA in the post from different angles, changing up the types of screens he sees and closing the angle of the screens so he can't split them.
label: analysis

text: Analysis like this is so refreshing compared to the usual garbage like 'one team wanted it more' or 'the Pacers didn't win, the Thunder just lost'.
label: hot_take

text: Makes the comments from Chuck's interview with Tyrese about Siakam's aggressiveness sound shallow when the real change was that the coaching staff put Siakam in a better position to succeed.
label: analysis

text: Thing is it's a much easier adjustment for the Pacers, who are used to having a high motion offense where lots of different guys take turns as the lead ball handler, compared to OKC where most of the team is almost always playing off of SGA.
label: analysis

text: Carlisle took lessons from the Warriors offense after seeing OKC guarding Hali like Steph Curry.
label: analysis

text: The biggest thing I've noticed is how much the Pacers change it up, even for plays that are working. OKC can't easily adjust if their plays aren't following the tape on them. On defense, SGA is the MVP for a reason. So the Pacers are giving him so many unexpected looks, such as the diagonal double team. That one little wrinkle throws him off a half second more because it breaks his expectation for where to find the open man.
label: analysis

text: There's an interesting OKC weakness on defense. They're so good on moving one step ahead of the action — anticipating the screen, giving help defense in the early shot clock on dead spots then quickly coming back — that it usually feels like they have 6 defenders. Indy is the first team to exploit this over helping. Hali attacks this specific point in the defense, turning their strength into weakness.
label: analysis

text: Carlisle has grown as a coach since being ousted from Dallas. He's done an unbelievable job elevating the talent of the Pacers. This team has been staging big comebacks and finding ways to manufacture points in critical moments, but also scheme defenses which gives his guys a chance.
label: analysis

text: Carlisle always has 'that guy' off the bench as a curveball. It was JJ, then it was Brunson, and now it's TJ.
label: analysis

text: Carlisle has a good track record of making game-to-game adjustments, like when he adjusted and Dallas won the last 3 games against the Heat in the 2011 Finals. The Mavs were underdogs, much like the Pacers were underdogs going into the OKC series.
label: analysis

text: That motion offense with the 3, 4, 5 high handoffs of the ball before someone attacks the basket with a drive or pass is gone. That was a staple of the first 3 series. It was 90% of the half court offense. Rick just switched it up seamlessly in the finals after game 1.
label: analysis

text: OKC defense weakness last year was their lack of size. Chet had a positive +/- in almost every game of a series they lost — the Mavs feasted on the fact that OKC had no backup big and couldn't handle physicality.
label: analysis

text: Don't put Caruso and Dort in for so many minutes together. You can't rely so much on SGA on offense, you need 2 other good scoring options. Rely too much on SGA and he becomes useless on defense.
label: analysis

text: This Pacers team has like 5 separate high energy 6th/7th man type players so the bench hangs in and sometimes wins when OKC is used to a hockey line change pushing a 5 point lead to 15.
label: analysis

text: Hand offs, run Hali off-ball whenever possible, exploit OKC's super aggressive defense by attacking the overhelp, get the ball out of Shai's hands.
label: analysis

text: Turner's play really stands out. Barely a play goes by without his spacing and screening opening things up for them. Haliburton's passing isn't as dazzling as Jokic's, but some of the transition passes show equally elite vision and skill.
label: analysis

text: Pushing the pace is one thing I wish the Wolves would have done. We had so much trouble once they got set, but we always walked the ball up the court.
label: analysis

text: Rick Carlisle's stock is insane. This team kinda reminds me of the championship Mavs with Dirk — how their entire roster contributes in unique and effective ways.
label: hot_take

text: Framing the Denver series as a 'one man show' is such lazy analysis.
label: hot_take
